summary: While software development is largely an art focused on architectural and design decisions, debugging and performance analysis require a scientific approach. Good debuggers treat problems methodically, forming hypotheses and testing assumptions rather than relying on trial and error. The author illustrates this through her own debugging practice of keeping lab-notebook-style notes and through a cautionary tale about performance tuning that failed due to lack of reproducible measurements. Only after establishing proper metrics could her team make confident progress on performance improvements.
key_points: Most application development decisions are judgment calls (art), but debugging and performance analysis require scientific method
Good debuggers believe in findable root causes and break down problems methodically rather than using trial and error
Performance tuning without reproducible measurements leads to guesswork and wasted time
Instinct in debugging is valuable but ultimately derives from building a foundation of scientific observation
Keeping notes resembling a lab notebook is essential for debugging complex distributed systems
money_quotes: Despite the study of computing being termed 'Computer Science', most working software developers will agree that development is more art than science.

Good debuggers, on the other hand, know that there is a root cause for errors that can be tracked down, and they are able to break down the problem in a methodical way, checking assumptions and observing behavior through repeated experiments and analysis.
I always believe that with enough patience and help I can in fact find the root of the problem, and I pretty much always do.
When I dig into a tough problem, I start keeping notes that resemble a slightly disorganized lab notebook.
We were looking for luck, but we need science, and our lack of scientific approach hampered our progress.
Good instincts start from science and cold, hard, repeatable, observable truths.
key_insight: Debugging and performance tuning require scientific rigor—forming hypotheses, creating reproducible experiments, and tracking observations—rather than the trial-and-error approach many developers default to.
